---
title: "ai-minerals"
subtitle: "A programmer learning to model where the next ore body might be — on public data, on my own time"
---

I'm a programmer. Thirty years of experience, twenty-five at Check Point
on Windows systems and security, and I'm now pivoting toward AI in
mineral exploration. This site is what I've built on my own time over
the last year or so: five projects, all on public data, mostly asking
some version of the same question — how much can a careful ML pipeline
really tell you about where to drill, and where does it break down? The
family-history reason I'm doing this at all is in the cover letter;
short version, three generations of mining engineers on my mother's
side, and once I started digging into family-claim records I kept
running into problems that turned out to be programming problems.

The chapters cover four within-region prospectivity pipelines,
reproductions of two well-known published methods (one of which doesn't
survive an honest audit as cleanly as its published numbers suggest), a
five-region transfer test, and two takes on the actual decision — given
a probability map, where do you put the next drill hole? Each chapter
has a longer notebook trail behind it — full data, code, methodology
choices, and what didn't work — at the
[review build](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/).

## Chapters

### [1. Bear Cub (Cape Nome placer Au)](bear_cub.qmd)

My family's claim on the Nome paleo-beach gold deposits — the project
that started everything. Twenty-four hand-written drill logs from
1919–1955 ingested with a multi-engine OCR pipeline (Tesseract, TrOCR,
Cloud Vision, Claude Opus arbitrating with structured-output JSON),
reconstructed into a 3D bedrock + pay-zone model, then resourced with
Voronoi-cell volumetrics and Monte Carlo uncertainty. It started as an
ongoing-litigation problem on the claim (high-grading by another
owner's lessees) and grew into a methodology I've reused since.

### [2. Regional MPM pipelines](regional.qmd)

Four within-region prospectivity pipelines, all built the same way:
USGS / NRCan / state-survey public data → Random Forest with 20 km
spatial-block CV → leak-free out-of-fold scoring with bootstrap
confidence intervals. The four regions: Eastern Alaska (porphyry-Cu),
BC Golden Triangle (multi-deposit), California Mother Lode
(orogenic-Au), Arizona (porphyry-Cu). Top-1% capture rates of 22–23%
across all four — about 22× better than random.

### [3. Reproducing + auditing two published methods](reproductions.qmd)

I cloned the official code for two well-known MPM papers and re-ran
them under honest validation. **Lawley 2022** (continental Zn-Pb,
published AUC 0.983) reproduces cleanly, but the published number
includes a label leak (2.4 pp), a 1-D vs 2-D spatial-blocking gap
(9.0 pp), and a cross-continent transfer collapse (~16 pp). Honest
AUC: 0.71–0.87. **DEEP-SEAM** (Curnamona REE, published 86% top-2%
capture) is measured under a 0-D random split with no spatial
blocking; the implementation also diverges from the paper text in two
places. I tried to find a charitable read of both and report what I
actually saw.

### [4. Cross-region experiments](cross_region.qmd)

A five-region transferability test for the DEEP-SEAM deviation-network
architecture. The 2–3× advantage over Random Forest that the paper
reports holds on its tuning dataset (Curnamona REE, 7 positives) and
nowhere else: Tanacross porphyry-Cu (45 positives), Arizona
porphyry-Cu (191 positives), Lawley continental MVT (2,027 positives,
2-D blocked CV), and US Western Carbonatite REE Belt (14 positives,
within-niche test) all favor classical tree-based methods, by 7 to 37
percentage points. Useful negative result.

### [5. Drill planning under uncertainty](drill_planning.qmd)

The downstream question: given a prospectivity map, where should the
next hole actually go? Two takes at different scales — a POMDP with
Monte Carlo Tree Search on the BCGT regional prior, plus a
Gaussian-process drill-site recommender on the Bear Cub placer
property. Different math, same job — turn a probability map into a
hole location, factoring in the uncertainty.

## Methodology in one paragraph

Here's the methodology in plain terms, with the caveat that I'm a
programmer, not a geologist or a research statistician — when I say
"the methodology" I mean "what the published literature does, and
what I've tried to do carefully over the past year."

Federal and state surveys publish a lot of mineral data: occurrence
records, geologic maps, magnetics and gravity, stream-sediment
geochemistry, satellite imagery. Resample everything onto a common
grid and each cell of land becomes a feature vector. Train a Random
Forest (or gradient-boosted trees, or a deeper neural net) to
recognize cells with known deposits, holding out spatial blocks so
the model can't cheat by memorizing neighbors. The output is a
per-cell score across the region: the model's guess at where the
unknown deposits are.

The part I've spent the most time on isn't the training, it's the
honesty check afterward. The methodology has four well-known traps —
inflated training positives, spatial leakage, exploration bias in
the labels, and cross-region transfer failure — and all four show up
in this portfolio. Chapter 3 is the most explicit about it: I
quantify how much of two published AUC numbers each trap accounts
for. But the same issues are flagged in the within-region work too
where I noticed them, and I'm sure I missed some.

## Stack

Public data sources: USGS, NRCan, state surveys, Sentinel-2. Code is
Python — scikit-learn, pandas + pyarrow, geopandas + rasterio,
PyTorch (for the DEEP-SEAM port), PyVista for the Bear Cub 3D
visualization, Quarto for the site itself.

## Site map

| Chapter | Detail |
|---|---|
| [1. Bear Cub](bear_cub.qmd) | [internal/bear_cub/main](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/main.html), [mining_review](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bear_cub/mining_review.html) |
| [2. Regional MPM](regional.qmd) | [Eastern Alaska](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.html), [BCGT](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bcgt/bcgt_porphyry_prospectivity.html), [Mother Lode](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/motherlode.html), [Arizona](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/arizona/main.html) |
| [3. Reproductions + audits](reproductions.qmd) | [Lawley](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/lawley.html), [Lawley audit](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/lawley_audit.html), [DEEP-SEAM](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/deep_seam_cross_region.html), [DEEP-SEAM audit](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/deep_seam_audit.html) |
| [4. Cross-region](cross_region.qmd) | [four-cell deep dive](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/cross_region/four_cell.html), [US Carbonatite REE](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/prospectus/us_carbonatite_ree.html) |
| [5. Drill planning](drill_planning.qmd) | [overview](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/index.html), [BCGT POMDP](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/bcgt/decision_planning.html), [Bear Cub GP](https://johnsondevco.com/plans/ai-minerals-internal/notebooks/drill_planning/bear_cub_gp_recommender.html) |

## Reproduce

```bash
git clone <this-repo>
cd ai-minerals
uv sync
quarto render
```

License: MIT.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from datetime import date
print(f"\n---\n\n*Site rendered {date.today().isoformat()}.*")
```
